Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines sources that fetch prebuilt tarballs from the official GitHub Releases page of the project (`https://github.com/jondkinney/mousehop`). These URLs are predictable and tied directly to a specific version (`v$pkgver`), with filenames that match the architecture. The `sha256sums` are explicitly defined and will be used by `makepkg` to verify the integrity of the downloaded files. No command substitutions, unquoted expressions, or dynamic code execution appear in the global scope or in a `pkgver()` function (which is absent). The variables used (`pkgver`, `_pkgname`, `CARCH`) are either static or standard environment variables handled safely within context. There is no `pkgver()` function to execute during sourcing, so version computation poses no risk. The `source` arrays for each architecture use hardcoded URLs with no dynamic fetching logic that could lead to remote code execution during download or extraction. Thus, running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` or `makepkg --printsrcinfo` only triggers safe operations: parsing static metadata and downloading verifiable upstream binaries.
</details>
<summary>
Safe sources and no dynamic code execution
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe sources and no dynamic code execution[/]
Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent...
makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful
Running makepkg --printsrcinfo to get source files for agent...
makepkg --printsrcinfo successful
Reviewing required files...
[0/1] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD retrieves prebuilt binaries from the official GitHub repository of the project, specifically from tagged releases (v$pkgver). The source URLs point directly to signed, versioned tarballs hosted at https://github.com/jondkinney/mousehop, which is the upstream source as declared in the package metadata. Checksums are present for both x86_64 and aarch64 architectures, although they are placeholders that are updated by CI via `updpkgsums`. This is a common and acceptable practice in AUR packages where automated tooling ensures checksums match the actual release assets.

The `package()` function extracts and installs files from the prebuilt archive into standard system directories: the binary to `/usr/bin`, desktop file and icon to appropriate shared locations, and license files to `/usr/share/licenses`. All operations are confined to the package directory (`$pkgdir`) and use standard, safe commands like `install` with explicit permission modes. No network operations, dynamic code evaluation (`eval`, base64-decoding), or runtime modifications are performed during installation.

There is no evidence of malicious behavior such as data exfiltration, use of obfuscated commands, or execution of external scripts. The package conflicts properly with other variants (`mousehop`, `mousehop-git`), and dependencies are appropriate for a GUI application using GTK (e.g., `libadwaita`, `gtk4`, `hicolor-icon-theme`). The build process does not involve compiling from source or fetching additional resources at build time, which is expected and acceptable for a `-bin` package distributing prebuilt binaries.
</details>
<summary>
Standard prebuilt binary package with safe installation; no malicious content found.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for com.mousehop.Mousehop.desktop:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `com.mousehop.Mousehop.desktop` is a standard desktop entry file used by desktop environments to integrate applications into the system menu. It declares metadata such as the application name, comment in multiple languages, icon, and execution command (`Exec=mousehop`). The content contains no executable code, scripts, or commands—only simple key-value pairs conforming to the Desktop Entry specification.

All fields are appropriate for the stated application purpose: sharing mouse and keyboard over LAN. The `Exec` field invokes `mousehop`, which is the expected launch command for the application and points to a binary installed in the system PATH. There are no network operations, file manipulations, or shell commands embedded in the file. The use of escaped ampersands (`&`) in comments is proper desktop file syntax and not an obfuscation technique.

This file poses no security risk. It is a benign configuration file used for desktop integration and contains no malicious or suspicious content.
</details>
<summary>
Benign desktop entry file, no executable code
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/3] Reviewing src/mousehop-0.14.2-x86_64/LICENSE, src/mousehop-0.14.2-x86_64/NOTICE...

LLM auditresponse for NOTICE:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The NOTICE file contains only copyright and licensing information for the Mousehop project, which is a fork of lan-mouse. It clearly states the original and modified works, their respective authors, and the licensing terms under GPL-3.0-or-later. There is no executable code, network activity, or system interaction present in this file. It is a standard textual notice file commonly found in open-source software distributions and poses no security risk.
</details>
<summary>
Licensing notice only, no code or malicious content</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/3] Reviewing src/mousehop-0.14.2-x86_64/LICENSE...
+ Reviewed src/mousehop-0.14.2-x86_64/NOTICE. Status: SAFE -- Licensing notice only, no code or malicious content
LLM auditresponse for LICENSE:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is the standard text of the GNU General Public License Version 3 (GPLv3), an open-source license published by the Free Software Foundation. It contains no executable code, scripts, or commands. It is a legal document that outlines the terms under which software may be copied, distributed, and modified. The presence of this file in a package, especially one named `mousehop-bin`, is normal and expected, particularly to indicate the licensing of accompanying software. There are no indicators of obfuscation, network activity, data exfiltration, or malicious behavior, as the file serves only an informational and legal purpose.
</details>
<summary>
Standard GPLv3 license text, no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This PKGBUILD contains no top-level or `pkgver()` code execution beyond standard variable assignments and string definitions. The `source` arrays point to GitHub release tarballs over HTTPS, which is the expected upstream for this package. No commands are run during sourcing that would download or execute untrusted payloads, nor are there any obfuscated or potentially dangerous constructs. The `package()` function is defined but will not be executed during the `--nobuild --noprepare` scope. Running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` to fetch sources and parse the PKGBUILD is safe.
</details>
<summary>
Standard PKGBUILD with no top-level execution risks.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is a standard AUR package file for a prebuilt binary release of the `mousehop` project. It fetches tarballs from the project's official GitHub releases URL (`https://github.com/jondkinney/$_pkgname/releases/download/v$pkgver/...`), which is the expected upstream source. The `package()` function performs routine installation of the binary, desktop file, icon, and license files from the tarball. No obfuscated code, no unexpected network connections (beyond the declared source downloads), no dangerous commands like `eval`, `curl`, `wget` in non-standard contexts, and no attempts to modify system files outside the package's scope. The checksums are provided (not `SKIP`) and are pinned to a specific release. There is no evidence of malicious or supply-chain attack behavior.
</details>
<summary>Standard AUR package for prebuilt binary release.</summary>
</security_assessment>
